chunk_summary: Guidelines for discharge readiness, rehabilitation, and high-risk surveillance planning in pediatric bone infections, emphasizing clinical improvement, structured follow-up, and preventive care strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand discharge readiness criteria for pediatric bone infections, including clinical and laboratory parameters.
Learn rehabilitation strategies to prevent complications like stiffness and ensure safe recovery.
Identify high-risk cases requiring structured surveillance and follow-up imaging.
Develop comprehensive outpatient care plans, including medication schedules and return precautions.
key_facts: Discharge readiness requires being afebrile for at least 48 hours, reduced pain, oral medication tolerance, and a downward trend in inflammatory markers.
Families must receive a written plan covering medication schedules, wound care, and return precautions for symptoms like fever or swelling.
Rehabilitation should balance early mobilization with pain and source control, including physiotherapy for range of motion and strength recovery.
High-risk cases (e.g., neonates, MRSA infections, spinal disease) may need specialist review and repeat imaging at 4-6 weeks.
Contact sports and impact activities should be avoided until pain resolves and bone healing is confirmed by the treating team.
reference_quote: Discharge should occur only when the child is clinically improving and the outpatient plan is concrete. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Discharge readiness criteria for pediatric bone infections
